elements.sent-2411-001: Reliably predicting nuclear properties across the entire chart of isotopes is important for applications ranging from nuclear astrophysics to superheavy science to nuclear technology.
elements.sent-2411-002: To this day, however, all the theoretical models that can scale at the level of the chart of isotopes remain semi phenomenological.
elements.sent-2411-003: Because they are fitted locally, their predictive power can vary significantly; different versions of the same theory provide different predictions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Bayesian model mixing takes advantage of such imperfect models to build a local mixture of a set of models to make improved predictions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Earlier attempts to use Bayesian model mixing for mass table calculations relied on models treated at single-reference energy density functional level, which fail to capture some of the correlations caused by configuration mixing or the restoration of broken symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this study we have applied Bayesian model mixing techniques within a multi-reference energy density functional (MR-EDF) framework.
elements.sent-2411-007: We considered predictions of two-particle separation energies from particle number projection or angular momentum projection with four different energy density functionals -- a total of eight different MR-EDF models.
elements.sent-2411-008: We used a hierarchical Bayesian stacking framework with a Dirichlet prior distribution over weights together with an inverse log-ratio transform to enable positive correlations between different models.
elements.sent-2411-009: We found that Bayesian model mixing provide significantly improved predictions over results from single MR-EDF calculations.
elements.sent-2411-010: Most of the nuclei in the nuclear chart are unstable making them difficult to study experimentally in the laboratory.
elements.sent-2411-011: Therefore one has to rely more on theoretical models for predicting their properties.
elements.sent-2411-012: Accurate predictions of nuclear properties are especially relevant to improve our understanding of the formation of elements in the Universe \cite{schatz2022horizons, mumpower2016impact,aprahamian2005nuclear and the performance of nuclear technologies \cite{henning2024need,kolos2022current .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In spite of constant progress, the complexity of nuclear interactions in many-body systems and the huge computational cost involved prevent ab initio approaches to be applied across the entire chart of isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-014: Conversely, mean-field based methods can be applied to any arbitrary nucleus but their more phenomenological nature lead to large uncertainties away from stability as predictions from different parametrizations of the mean field differ greatly from one another \cite{sobiczewski2018detailed .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, one can leverage the combined knowledge from these different models together with available experimental data to get better predictions.
elements.sent-2411-016: Statistical methods belonging to the broader field of artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) are well suited to this task \cite{boehnlein2022colloquium .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Over the last decade, there have been many efforts to use AI/ML tools to directly predict basic nuclear properties, e.g., nuclear binding energies \cite{li2024atomic,yuksel2024nuclear,niu2022nuclear,sharma2022learning, mumpower2022physically,lovell2022nuclear,niu2018nuclear , radii \cite{dong2022novel , $\beta$-decay rates \cite{niu2019predictions or even fission product yields \cite{wang2019bayesian .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Since most AI/ML methods are data driven and data in unstable nuclei are rather sparse, such direct AI/ML extrapolations of observables spanning a large range of values could become unwieldy.
elements.sent-2411-019: A more robust approach is to use AI/ML techniques to learn corrections to physics models, e.g., the discrepancy between theoretical predictions from these physics models and experimental measurements \cite{navarroperez2022controlling, utama2018validating,utama2017refining,utama2016nucleara .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The machine learning community has developed many ensemble-learning methods to tackle the case where several competing models have similar predictive power \cite{mienye2022survey,rokach2010ensemblebased .}
elements.sent-2411-021: For example, Bayesian model averaging (BMA) provides a natural Bayesian framework for combining model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-022: It has been applied in different fields of applications \cite{fragoso2018bayesian,hinne2020conceptual including various nuclear physics problems \cite{neufcourt2020quantified,neufcourt2019neutron, saito2024uncertainty,qiu2024bayesian .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: BMA provide a weighted average of the given models by assigning them weights according to their posterior probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, such weights are global: BMA ignores the local variations in the prediction accuracy of different models.
elements.sent-2411-025: Bayesian model mixing (BMM) strategies have been suggested to account for these local effects \cite{phillips2021get,semposki2022interpolating,giuliani2024model, yannotty2024model .}
elements.sent-2411-026: A recent study demonstrated BMM's effectiveness in predicting two-neutron separation energies \cite{kejzlar2023local .}
elements.sent-2411-027: By employing a Dirichlet distribution to infer model weights and assigning them hierarchically based on the model input space to account for variations in the fidelity of different models, this approach outperformed BMA in terms of uncertainty quantification and predictions.
elements.sent-2411-028: It also accounted for the correlations between the model weights for a given nucleus and from different parts of the nuclear chart.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, the use of a Dirichlet prior and the simplex constraint imposed on the weights also make this approach less flexible by neglecting potential positive correlations between the model weights for a given nucleus.
elements.sent-2411-030: The limitations of simplex distributions like the Dirichlet are well studied, and various alternative approaches have been developed to address them \cite{ongaro2020new, greenacre2023aitchisons,aitchison1982statistical,aitchison2003statistical .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In addition, parametrizing model weights only as a function of proton and neutron numbers (input features) can lead to unreliable extrapolations \cite{navarroperez2022controlling .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, all attempts to predict two-neutron separation energies using BMA and BMM have so far relied on combining results from different energy functionals treated at the single-reference energy density functional (SR-EDF) level \cite{neufcourt2020quantified,neufcourt2019neutron,giuliani2024model, kejzlar2023local .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, predictions at the SR-EDF level have limited accuracy and are typically unable to describe the transition from well deformed to (doubly) closed-shell nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-034: Increasing the predictive power of these methods requires working within the multi-reference energy density functional (MR-EDF) framework, which allows incorporating additional correlations effects that cannot be captured at the SR-EDF level \cite{schunck2019energy .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Currently, it is computationally prohibitive to restore all broken symmetries at once and simultaneously perform configuration mixing.
elements.sent-2411-036: BMM might provide a more economical method to explore the effect of restoring different symmetries: restoring one symmetry at a time, for example, yield a small set of different theoretical models that can be mixed together in a BMM framework.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this study, we use Bayesian model mixing on two-particle separation energies with models based on particle-number and angular momentum projection of Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov solutions with Skyrme energy density functionals.
elements.sent-2411-038: To ensure the robustness of extrapolations, we parametrize the BMM weights with several different features that have similar probability distribution in the training and testing region.
elements.sent-2411-039: We also investigate the use of the inverse log-ratio transform \cite{aitchison1982statistical to represent model weights, allowing for the incorporation of positive correlations between them.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In section \ref{sec:theory we give a succinct summary of nuclear mass table calculations with MR-EDF methods.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section \ref{sec:bmm presents our implementation of Bayesian model mixing and discusses to express the weights of the models to allow positive correlations among them.}
elements.section-2411-002: Mass Table Calculations with Projection Methods
elements.sent-2411-043: The goal of this work was to explore the possibility to capture beyond mean-field correlations in a statistical mixing model.
elements.sent-2411-044: As well known, such correlations are the main reason why there exist systematic deviations between theoretical predictions and experimental values of nuclear binding energies near closed shells (``arches'') \cite{bender2004correlation,bender2006global, klupfel2009variations .}
elements.sent-2411-045: They are naturally incorporated in multi-reference energy density functional (MR-EDF) methods, which include among others symmetry restoration and configuration mixing, e.g., with the generator coordinate method \cite{bender2003selfconsistent,schunck2019energy .}
elements.sent-2411-046: While earlier studies pointed to the leading role of quadrupole shape mixing, we focused here on the restoration of broken symmetries -- particle number and angular momentum.
elements.sent-2411-047: Both particle number projection (PNP) and angular momentum projection (AMP) were based off axially-symmetric Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov (HFB) solutions with Skyrme pseudopotentials.
elements.sent-2411-048: Applying either PNP or AMP systematically lowers the nuclear binding energy compared to the HFB reference: $E_{\rm proj \leq E_{\rm HFB}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this exploratory study, we did not refit the coupling constants of the energy density functional (EDF).
elements.sent-2411-050: Since all the EDFs considered were adjusted at the single-reference level, the correlation energy brought by the projections thus introduces a systematic bias to the theoretical prediction.
elements.sent-2411-051: For example, let us assume a somewhat idealistic case where we had a parametrization $\mathcal{P}$ of the Skyrme EDF fitted in such a way that the residual $x \equiv E_{\rm HFB - E_{\rm exp.}$ (approximately) followed a normal distribution $x \sim \mathcal{N (0,\sigma)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Then, the mean of the ``projected'' residual $x_{\rm proj \equiv E_{\rm proj - E_{\rm exp.}$ would most likely be $\bar{x _{\rm proj < 0$ (and, of course, the distribution may not be Gaussian anymore).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: This is a well known effect that restoring broken symmetries, or doing configuration mixing, {\em without refitting the parameters of the functional often degrades the quality of the results even though the theory is more advanced.}
elements.sent-2411-054: For this reason, rather than take our data as the nuclear binding energy, we chose instead the two-particle separation energy.
elements.sent-2411-055: All results will be presented for the two-neutron separation energy $S_{2n = B(N) - B(N-2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: It is expected that the bias introduced by the projection will largely cancel out when taking the difference between two neighboring nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-057: We calculated eight mass tables corresponding to angular momentum projection and particle number projection with the HFB1 \cite{schunck2015error , SkM* \cite{bartel1982better , SkI3 \cite{reinhard1995nuclear , and SLy4 \cite{chabanat1998skyrme parameterizations of the Skyrme functional.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The SLy4, SkM* and SkI3 parametrizations only apply to the particle-hole channel; in the particle-particle channel, we used a standard zero-range, surface-volume density-dependent pairing force of the type $V(\gras{r ) = \Vq \left( 1 - \tfrac{1 {2 \tfrac{\rho(\gras{r ) {\rho_{c \right)$ with $\Vq$ the pairing strength for particle $q\equiv n,p$ and $\rho_{c = 0.16$ fm$^{-3}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Because of the zero-range of the pairing force, a cutoff of $E_{\rm cut = 60$ MeV on quasiparticle energies was included when computing densities.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The pairing strengths of protons and neutrons were fitted on the 3-point odd-even mass difference in $^{232}$Th and are listed in Table~\ref{tab:pairing_strengths .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Adopted pairing strengths $V_0^q$ in MeV $\cdot$ fm$^3$ for the Skyrme EDFs used in this work. They were fitted to reproduce the three-point odd-even mass staggering in $^{232}$Th. } \label{tab:pairing_strengths} \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{ccccc} SLy4 SkM* SkI3 $V_0^n$ -300.213 -260.946 -351.828 $V_0^p$ -336.292 -321.045 -376.039 \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
elements.sent-2411-061: All the nuclear mass tables used in this study were calculated with the HFBTHO code \cite{marevic2022axiallydeformed .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The procedure was analogue to the one used, e.g., in \cite{sprouse2020propagation,navarroperez2022controlling : for each nucleus, we first determined the constrained HFB solution for five different values of the axial quadrupole moment spanning both oblate, spherical and prolate shapes.}
elements.sent-2411-063: We then restarted the calculation without the constraint and retained as HFB ground-state solution the one with the lowest energy.
elements.sent-2411-064: The mass tables were calculated for all even even nuclei between $Z=2$ to $Z=120$ and the two-particle separation energy was the criterion to identify the driplines.
elements.sent-2411-065: Given the HFB mass table, we then apply either PNP or AMP to obtain the projected solution.
elements.sent-2411-066: For PNP, we used $N_{\phi = 9$ gauge points while for AMP we used $N_{\beta = 30$ rotational angles.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Low-energy nuclear theory is a prime example where multiple models describing the same physical system, each based on different assumptions and approximations, can have very similar predictive power.
elements.sent-2411-068: While selecting the best performing model is a common practice, this approach does not fully leverage the collective knowledge of these models.
elements.sent-2411-069: Furthermore if all models are incorrect, relying solely on the top performer can still lead to misleading predictions.
elements.sent-2411-070: In statistical learning, model sets can be categorized into three classes: $\mathcal{M}$-closed, $\mathcal{M}$-complete, and $\mathcal{M}$-open \cite{le2017bayes .}
elements.sent-2411-071: If one of the models within the set is the true data generating model ($M_\dagger$), such model set is considered $\mathcal{M}$-closed and $M_\dagger$ can be selected based on certain criteria or experimental evidence.
elements.sent-2411-072: If $M_\dagger$ is not in the model set but can be conceptualized using existing knowledge, even if practically inaccessible, the set is called $\mathcal{M}$-complete.
elements.sent-2411-073: Finally, a setting is called $\mathcal{M}$-open if $M_\dagger$ is inconceivable and all models in the set approach it without being able to represent it because of knowledge or resource limitations.
elements.sent-2411-074: The problem being studied in this paper falls in the $\mathcal{M}$-open class due to our lack of knowledge the properties of the nuclear many-body system.
elements.sent-2411-075: Model averaging methods are well suited for $\mathcal{M}$-open problems as they provide predictions by combining different models and assigning them varying weights \cite{yao2022bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-076: While Bayesian Model Averaging (BMA) already outperforms individual models, it cannot take into account the fact that different models can perform better in different region of the model's input space.
elements.sent-2411-077: Bayesian Model Mixing (BMM) strategies are much better at capturing local model fidelity since they assign weights that vary as a function of the model inputs \cite{semposki2022interpolating,giuliani2024model, yannotty2024model .}
elements.sent-2411-078: In this paper we use a hierarchical Bayesian stacking method similar to what was proposed in Ref.~\cite{kejzlar2023local with some modifications that we discuss below.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Let us consider a set of $K$ models denoted by $M_k$ with $k=1,2,...,K$ that predict the same physical quantity.
elements.sent-2411-080: We assume that we have $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$ experimental observations $y_i$ of this quantity.
elements.sent-2411-081: In a Bayesian stacking framework, we seek to build a meta model $\metaM$ as a linear combination of the given models.
elements.sent-2411-082: This linear combination is parametrized by a vector $\gras{x}$ of $p$ input features, $\metaM \equiv \metaM(\gras{x )$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: At each point $i$, we have different realizations of this input vector so that we can write \begin{equation}y_i \simeq \metaM(\gras{x}_i) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k(\gras{x}_i)M_k(\gras{x}_i)+\sigma\epsilon_i \label{eq:BMM}\end{equation} where $w_k(\gras{x _i)$ are the model weights.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The observational error $\epsilon_i$ is assumed to follow a unit normal distribution, $\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N (0,1)$, and $\sigma$ is a scaling parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Here the weights of each model $M_k$ obey the simplex condition \begin{equation}\sum_k w_k(\gras{x}_i) = 1, \qquad w_k(\gras{x}_i) \geq 0 \label{eq:weights}\end{equation} Now the goal of the problem is to find a suitable representation for the input-dependent model weights $w_k(\gras{x _i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: To create a locally weighted mixture of the participating models $M_k$ while ensuring that the simplex constraint \eqref{eq:weights is satisfied, we model the weights with a Dirichlet distribution, \begin{equation}p(\gras{w}|\gras{\alpha}) \sim \prod_{k=1}^{K} w_k^{\alpha_k-1}\end{equation} At each experimental observation $i$, the Dirichlet parameters $\gras{\alpha = ( \alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_{K )$ are functions of the input features $\gras{x}$ through: $\ln \alpha_k(\gras{x _i) = \gamma_k(\gras{x _i)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: This dependency enables the weights to vary across different input regions, thereby capturing potential correlations between them at different locations \cite{kejzlar2023local .}
elements.sent-2411-088: In this work, we explored two methods to represent $\gamma_k(\gras{x )$:.}
elements.sent-2411-089: In this case, we set $\gamma_k(\gras{x ) = \gras{\beta _k \cdot \gras{x}$, where $\gras{\beta _k$ is a vector of fitting parameters of length $p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: In this case, $\gamma_k(\gras{x )$ is a Gaussian process over the input features as \begin{equation}\gamma_k(\gras{x}) \sim \mathrm{GP}(\mu_k(\gras{x}),C_k(\gras{x},\gras{x}')) \label{eq:gp}\end{equation} The kernel $C_k(\gras{x ,\gras{x ')$ represents the covariance between the values of $\gamma_k$ at two different input locations, $\gras{x}$ and $\gras{x '$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The mean function $\mu_k(\gras{x )$ is the expected value of $\gamma_k$ and will control the model weights far from the training data \cite{kejzlar2023local .}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Since our input features are nuclear properties, the covariance kernel effectively captures the correlations between these properties in nuclei with different neutron and proton numbers.
elements.sent-2411-093: In this study, we have used constant mean functions $\mu_k$ and exponential quadratic kernel, \begin{equation}C_k(\gras{x},\gras{x}') = \eta_k \prod_{n=1}^{p} \exp\left[ \frac{-(x_{n}-x'_{n})^2}{2l_n^2} \right], \label{eq:cov_kernel}\end{equation} where $\eta_k$ are the variance parameter which scales the uncertainties associated with the Gaussian process predictions, and $l_n$ is the correlation length corresponding to the input feature $n$, which control the spatial range over which the properties of the nucleus are correlated.
elements.sent-2411-094: In both cases, the $\gamma_k$ function effectively models the correlations between the weights of different models corresponding to different nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-095: Concurrently, the Dirichlet distribution captures the correlations between weights of the same nucleus.
elements.sent-2411-096: The model weights $w_k$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:BMM live in a simplex sample space $\mathbb{S ^K$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: This is the reason why they are modeled using a Dirichlet prior distribution, since it is specifically designed for such spaces.
elements.sent-2411-098: However, Dirichlet distributions also enforce negative correlations among the weights.
elements.sent-2411-099: In some cases, it might be more desirable for the weights to be positively correlated, especially when multiple models perform well in similar regions.
elements.sent-2411-100: Furthermore, the constraints imposed by the simplex space make it challenging to directly interpret the covariance structure of the weights.
elements.sent-2411-102: Aitchison proposed transforming the simplex-constrained variables to real space $\mathbb{R ^{K-1}$ using log-ratio transformations to address these limitations \cite{aitchison1984log,aitchison1982statistical .}}
elements.sent-2411-103: In this study, we employed the additive log-ratio ({\ALR ) transformation, where the weights are transformed as \begin{equation}\omega_k = \ln\left(\frac{w_k}{w_K}\right), \qquad k=1,2,\dots,K-1\end{equation} where $w_K$ serves as a reference category against which ratios are defined; this choice is arbitrary and does not affect the analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The inverse {\ALR transformation is given by \begin{equation}w_k=\begin{cases} \frac{1}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K-1}\big( 1 + e^{\omega_{k'}} \big)}, \qquad k=K \frac{e^{\omega_{k'}}}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K-1} \big(1 + e^{\omega_{k'}}\big)}, \qquad k=1,2,..,K-1 \end{cases} \label{eq:inv_alr}\end{equation} A multivariate normal distribution $\mathcal{N ^{K-1 (\gras{\mu ,\Sigma)$ can induce a much richer distribution on $\mathbb{S ^K$ by exploiting the inverse-{\ALR \cite{aitchison1985practical .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: In this study we sampled the vector of transformed weights $\gras{\omega = (\omega_1,\dots,\omega_{K-1 )$ from such a multivariate normal distribution, \begin{equation}p(\gras{\omega}|\gras{\gamma}) \sim \mathcal{N}^{K-1}(\gras{\gamma}(\gras{x}),\Sigma), \qquad k=1,2,...,K-1\end{equation} where the components $\gamma_k$ of the vector $\gras{\gamma}$ were computed using either the generalized linear method or Gaussian process as discussed in the previous section.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The prior distribution for the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ was modeled with LKJ-distributed correlations \cite{lewandowski2009generating .}
elements.sent-2411-107: After sampling the $\omega_k$, the model weights are subsequently obtained using Eq. \eqref{eq:inv_alr .}
elements.sent-2411-108: In this section, we summarize the results of our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-109: We give the full details of the numerical setup employed to training the BMM before analyzing the global performance of the mixture model and the particular case of the Lead isotopic chain.
elements.sent-2411-110: We also discuss the empirical coverage probability of the weights and show how they vary across the nuclear chart.
elements.sent-2411-111: We performed BMM for two-neutron separation energies $S_{2n}$ using predictions from eight mass models for even-even nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-112: We used four different types of BMM models: the Generalised Linear Dirichlet model (GLD), the Generalised Linear Log-Ratio model (GLLR), the Gaussian Process Dirichlet model (GPD) and the Gaussian Process Log-Ratio model (GPLR).
elements.sent-2411-113: For training the models, we used $S_{2n}$ data from the 2020 Atomic Mass Evaluation (AME2020) \cite{wang2021ame for even-even nuclei with $Z\geq 8$ and $N\geq 8$.}
elements.sent-2411-114: In each mass table, MR-EDF calculations were not converged for a small subset of nuclei, which were excluded from further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-115: These included mostly spherical or near-spherical nuclei near doubly closed shells, where angular momentum projection could become numerically unwieldy.
elements.sent-2411-116: In total, $S_{2n}$ data for 624 even-even nuclei was included for both training and validation.
elements.sent-2411-117: We used a 80:20 ratio for training and validation by randomly sampling the validation set from the available data as presented in Fig. \ref{fig:training .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{traintest.pdf} \caption{Location of nuclei with available experimental data included in this study with the training set marked with black squares and the validation set by red squares.} \label{fig:training}
elements.sent-2411-118: The weights of the BMM were parametrized as a function of four input features: proton number, neutron number, total pairing energy and axial quadrupole deformation parameter $\beta_2$.
elements.sent-2411-119: The distribution of both the total pairing energy and the $\beta_2$ deformation parameter is similar in both training and testing regions.
elements.sent-2411-120: Such input features should help produce more reliable predictions far from the training region \cite{navarroperez2022controlling .}
elements.sent-2411-121: For each mass model, the total pairing energy and deformation parameter values were obtained from the HFB solution with the corresponding energy density functional.
elements.sent-2411-122: Because all four input features have different range of values, they were normalized between 0 and 1.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{hist_all_new.pdf} \caption{Histograms of the prediction error for each of the eight initial models and the BMM predictions, for each of the four types of BMM considered in this work: a) Generalized Linear Dirichlet, GLD; b) Generalized Linear Log-Ratio, GLLR; c) Gaussian Process Dirichlet, GPD; d) Gaussian Process Log-Ratio, GPLR.} \label{fig:predictions}
elements.sent-2411-123: The prediction errors of each mass model are heteroskedastic in nature because the variance differs across regions of the nuclear chart.
elements.sent-2411-124: To account for this, we scaled the error term in \eqref{eq:BMM as $\sigma = \kappa/A^{2/3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: In the training, all independent statistical parameters were assigned the same prior distributions or initial values across all variants of BMM models:.
elements.sent-2411-126: For the generalized linear method, the $\beta_k$ parameters ($\beta_k^1,\beta_k^2,...\beta_k^p$) were drawn from a unit normal distribution $\mathcal{N (0,1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-127: The scaling factor $\kappa$ of the error was drawn from a half-normal prior with unit variance.
elements.sent-2411-128: The parameters of the covariance kernel of Eq.~\eqref{eq:cov_kernel of the Gaussian processes were drawn from an independent $\Gamma$ prior distribution with both shape and rate parameters set to one.}
elements.sent-2411-129: This configuration results in a prior distribution with a mean and variance of one, which is equivalent to an exponential distribution.
elements.sent-2411-130: The constant mean functions $\mu_k(\gras{x )$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:gp were also drawn from normal priors $\mathcal{N (0,1)$.}}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Root mean square (r.m.s.) deviations for each of the initial models (first eight rows) and the four variant of the mixing model (last four rows). Results are divided into training, validation and total set. Units are MeV.} \label{tab:rmsd_values} \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{ccccc} Model.No. Model Training Validation Total 1 HFB1+PNP 1.136 1.032 1.116 2 HFB1+AMP 1.084 1.101 1.087 3 SKM*+PNP 1.229 1.193 1.222 4 SKM*+AMP 1.534 1.136 1.463 5 SKI3+PNP 1.202 1.195 1.201 6 SKI3+AMP 1.333 1.349 1.336 7 SLY4+PNP 0.977 1.015 0.985 8 SLY4+AMP 1.184 1.254 1.198 9 GLD 0.736 0.680 0.725 10 GLLR 0.749 0.684 0.736 11 GPD 0.440 0.606 0.478 12 GPLR 0.448 0.616 0.486 \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
elements.sent-2411-131: At the SR-EDF level, the average r.m.s.
elements.sent-2411-133: Since we do not refit the EDFs, predictions for $S_{2n}$ are less accurate than those from SR-EDF calculations: the average r.m.s.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, the standard deviation has decreased, from approximately 200 keV at the SR-EDF level to 139 keV at the MR-EDF level.
elements.sent-2411-135: These numbers indicate that no model significantly outperforms the others and that the overall predictive power is similar.
elements.sent-2411-136: However, the histograms of Fig.~\ref{fig:predictions also suggest that locally (= for a given nucleus $Z$ and $N$), the predictions can vary significantly between models.}
elements.sent-2411-137: From a BMM perspective, this scenario can be helpful because stacked averages can produce better results when the contributing models are more dissimilar or distinct \cite{le2017bayes, breiman1996stacked,clarke2003comparing Figure \ref{fig:predictions shows that the BMM models provides consistent results, with prediction residuals clearly centered around zero.. The r.m.s.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Both forms of the generalized linear model (GLD and GLLR) for the weights give similar results with an r.m.s.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Pred82_all_new.pdf} \caption{Prediction error of the two-neutron separation energy in Lead isotopes from the proton to the neutron dripline for each of the four BMM models: a) Generalized Linear Dirichlet, GLD; b) Generalized Linear Log-Ratio, GLLR; c) Gaussian Process Dirichlet, GPD; d) Gaussian Process Log-Ratio, GPLR.} \label{fig:isotopes_pb}
elements.sent-2411-139: Figure \ref{fig:isotopes_pb illustrates the performance of different BMM models by showing the prediction error $\Delta S_{2n = S_{2n ^{(\rm the.) - S_{2n ^{(\rm exp.)}$ on the two-neutron results for Pb isotopes both for the BMM and for each of the 8 individual ones.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: Overall, all BMM models give similar results with similar uncertainty bounds, whether they are based on the Dirichlet distribution or the log-ratio method.
elements.sent-2411-141: Because PNP or AMP are applied without refitting the underlying functional, $S_{2n}$ predictions often exhibit significant deviations from experimental data, leading to abrupt peaks in the prediction residual plots.
elements.sent-2411-142: BMM effectively mitigates these effects, especially when they are built with Gaussian processes, resulting in significantly improved predictions.
elements.sent-2411-143: Such Gaussian process models also reproduce experimental data better than Generalised linear methods because they model the weights as a nonlinear function of the input variables.
elements.sent-2411-144: Therefore we recommend using a nonlinear function to model the functional relation between weights and input variables.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{wsd82all_new.pdf} \caption{Posterior weights of each model in the BMM along the Lead isotopic line from the proton to the neutron dripline. a) Generalized Linear Dirichlet, GLD; b) Generalized Linear Log-Ratio, GLLR; c) Gaussian Process Dirichlet, GPD; d) Gaussian Process Log-Ratio, GPLR.} \label{fig:weights_pb}
elements.sent-2411-145: We show in Fig.~\ref{fig:weights_pb the mean value and 1$\sigma$ uncertainty band of the posterior weights of each of the eight models for the full Pb isotopic sequence from the proton to the neutron dripline.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Although the model weights learned using Dirichlet and log-ratio methods take similar values, their dependency on neutron number is different.
elements.sent-2411-147: In the case of the Dirichlet model -- panels (a) and (b) -- the weights vary much more rapidly as a function of $N$ than in the case of the GP model -- panels (c) and (d).
elements.sent-2411-148: In addition, these variations quickly disappear in the GP case as soon as the neutron number is outside the training region (at $N=132$).
elements.sent-2411-149: This is typical of the standard ``revert to the mean'' behavior of GP-based extrapolations.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Corr82all_new.pdf} \caption{Correlation matrix of the eight model weights in $^{208}$Pb. Dirichlet models are shown in panels (a) (Generalized Linear, GLD) and (c) (Gausian Process, GPD); models based on the inverse log-ratio transformation are shown in panels (b) (Generalized Linear, GLLR) and (d) (Gaussian Process, GPLR).} \label{fig:correlations_pb}
elements.sent-2411-150: It was mentioned in Sec~\ref{subsec:alr that Dirichlet prior distributions enforce negative correlations among the weights of the BMM while the {\ALR also allows for positive ones.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Strictly speaking, the Dirichlet distribution is the conjugate prior of the multinomial and categorical distributions.
elements.sent-2411-152: Since our likelihood follows a normal distribution, the posterior distribution of the weights will not remain exactly Dirichlet but somewhat similar.
elements.sent-2411-153: In practice, it manifests itself by the fact that model weights can have small positive correlations.
elements.sent-2411-154: This is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:correlations_pb for the case of $^{208}$Pb.}
elements.sent-2411-155: The figure shows the correlation matrix between the weights of each model for this nucleus.
elements.sent-2411-156: In the case of the Dirichlet method -- panels (a) and (c), all off-diagonal terms are either negative numbers or positive numbers smaller than 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-157: In contrast, the log-ratio method does not enforce any limitation on the sign of the correlations between model weights and can lead to positive correlations as high as +0.7.
elements.sent-2411-158: In fact, one observes that weight correlations in the log-ratio method are on average stronger than in the Dirichlet method.
elements.sent-2411-159: This observation is true not only for the Lead isotopic chain but for most nuclei in the nuclear chart.
elements.sent-2411-160: Following \cite{kejzlar2023local , we also computed the empirical coverage probability (ECP) as a function of the credibility interval to check the uncertainty representation of different BMM models \cite{gneiting2007probabilistic,gneiting2007strictly .}}
elements.sent-2411-161: We recall that the ECP quantifies the quality of the uncertainties associated with a given statistical model.
elements.sent-2411-162: For a credibility interval of $X \%$ -- the interval with the $X \%$ of the posterior distribution such that it leave equal probability density at both ends of the distribution, the value of the ECP estimates how many testing samples ($Y \%$ in percentage) fall into this interval: if $Y>X$, the BMM model is too conservative; if $Y<X$, the model underestimates uncertainties.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{coverage.pdf} \caption{Empirical coverage probability as a function of credibility level corresponding to the four different types of Bayesian mixing models: Generalized Linear Dirichlet (GLD), Generalized Linear Log-Ratio (GLLR), Gaussian Process Dirichlet (GPD), and Gaussian Process Log-Ratio (GPLR).} \label{fig:coverage}
elements.sent-2411-163: Figure \ref{fig:coverage shows the ECP for the four types of BMMs considered in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-164: We first notice that within any given family of BMM, i.e., Gaussian or linear, the ECP gives similar values irrespective of whether we use the {\ALR transformation or not.}
elements.sent-2411-165: However, predictions corresponding to Gaussian process methods are significantly more conservative than with Generalized linear methods.
elements.sent-2411-166: This could also be seen from Fig.~\ref{fig:weights_pb : for predictions far from the training region, the uncertainties associated with the value of the weights increase significantly for GPD and GPLR models.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{all_weightsLinearAlr.pdf} \caption{Posterior weight means of each of the eight models for the generalized linear BMM model with the inverse Log-ratio transform (GLLR). } \label{fig:posterior_gllr}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{all_weightsGpAlr.pdf} \caption{Same as Fig.\ref{fig:posterior_gllr} for the Gaussian process model with the inverse Log-ratio transform (GPLR).} \label{fig:posterior_gplr}
elements.sent-2411-167: We show the mean posterior weights of each model in the BMM across the entire nuclear chart in the case of the two BMM models with the Log-Ratio method, which has both the lowest complexity and smallest number of degrees of freedom and allows for correlations of arbitrary sign between model weights.
elements.sent-2411-168: Figure \ref{fig:posterior_gllr shows the weights for the Generalized linear model and Fig.~\ref{fig:posterior_gplr for the Gaussian process model.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Even though all the 8 models entering the BMM have similar predictive power, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:predictions and Table \ref{tab:rmsd_values , some of them clearly dominate, most notably model 1 (HFB1+PNP) and 3 (SkM*+PNP).}}
elements.sent-2411-170: We note that that the GLLR method extrapolates learned trends far from the training region.
elements.sent-2411-171: In addition, Table \ref{tab:rmsd_values shows that the performance of this method is in fact a little better in the validation region.}
elements.sent-2411-172: This somewhat counterintuitive result is caused by the fact that the validation region proportionally contains more light nuclei than the training one.
elements.sent-2411-173: Because HFB calculations are not very precise in light nuclei, the improvement brought by the BMM is much more significant.
elements.sent-2411-174: Finally, Fig.~\ref{fig:coverage shows that the ECP ratio is very close to the diagonal, suggesting a near-ideal estimate of uncertainties.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Taken together, these observations suggest that GLLR models should provide robust estimates of separation energies.
elements.sent-2411-176: Conversely, GPLR methods perform much better in the training region than in the validation region as shown in Table \ref{tab:rmsd_values .}
elements.sent-2411-177: At the same time, the weights quickly converge towards a constant value as we move away from the training set, leading into a loss of structure in each of the panels in Fig.~\ref{fig:posterior_gplr .}
elements.sent-2411-178: These behavior are typical of overfitting and suggest that including robust input features in the parameterization of the weights was not sufficient to ensure reliable extrapolations.
elements.sent-2411-179: This observation is reinforced by the much larger value of the ECP ratio.
elements.sent-2411-180: In this work, we performed a Bayesian model mixing (BMM) of eight different mass models to make predictions of two-particle separation energies.
elements.sent-2411-181: In contrast to similar studies performed so far, our mass models were based on multi-reference energy density functional calculations, namely particle-number and angular momentum projection of Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov solutions with Skyrme energy functionals.
elements.sent-2411-182: BMM weights were parametrized not only as a function of proton and neutron numbers but also as a function of the total pairing energy and axial quadrupole deformation in order to improve their robustness in extrapolations.
elements.sent-2411-183: To enhance the flexibility of the mixing model while reducing its complexity, we applied an inverse ALR transformation to model weights as function of input features.
elements.sent-2411-184: We found that BMM is effective in combining results from different symmetry restoration schemes, reducing the prediction error by a factor ranging from 40\% to 60\% depending on the version of the BMM.
elements.sent-2411-185: Employing the inverse {\ALR transformation gives similar performance as the traditional Dirichlet distribution method, while requiring fewer fitting parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-186: It also produces more correlated posterior weights by allowing for positive correlations between them.
elements.sent-2411-187: While modeling BMM weights as a Gaussian process over input variables allows reducing the r.m.s.
elements.sent-2411-188: This could be mitigated by fitting the parameters of the energy functional concurrently with the weights of the BMM.
